subject: English
period_schedule_display:
Period schedule:
  Row 1: 40 minutes × 4 periods = 160 minutes
Total: 4 periods · 2h 40min

periods.activity_title: Upside-Down World: Reading and Discussing the Nonsense Poem

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Identify true statements about the funny man from a list
Complete sentences on tone, rhyme scheme, and repetition of 'funny'
Choose correct answers on imagery, structure, and alliteration in the poem

phases.description: Teacher reads the poem aloud expressively, modelling the absurdist tone, then invites students to name what makes it funny — shoe on head, hats on feet, currant bun as a rose.

Students work in pairs to identify the true statements from the list of six (page 61), checking answers with the teacher. The four true statements — funny man wore two hats on his feet, hopped home on his head, smiled at the poet, and the poet never heard such an amusing song — are discussed.

Students complete the three-part sentence completion task on tone, rhyme scheme, and the function of 'funny' (page 61), then answer the three MCQ items on imagery, structure, and alliteration (page 61).
Teacher briefly explains the ABCB rhyme scheme by marking it on the board and previews the extract analysis for Period 8.

periods.teacher_notes: The poem_appreciation_summary identifies the ABCB rhyme scheme and the synesthetic oxymorons ('funny sounding sight', 'funny looking smell') as the poem's key literary features; anchor the discussion of tone and repetition to these. Ensure students understand 'alliteration' before the MCQ task.

periods.activity_title: Extract Analysis and a Funny Personal Incident

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Analyse Extract 1 — the currant bun presented as a rose
Analyse Extract 2 — the funny man hops home on his head
Select picture related to the girl's narration of a funny incident
Arrange events from the girl's narration in order of occurrence

phases.description: Students read Extract 1 closely and answer the four sub-items: why the funny man addresses the poet as 'Your Highness', choosing the phrase showing a polite request, identifying a currant bun from images, and completing the sentence about the poet's 'Well!' of surprise (page 62).

Students read Extract 2 and answer the four sub-items: identifying the line that tells us the sound was unique, completing the sentence about why the poet calls the man 'my friend', explaining what the funny man's reaction reveals, and choosing the feeling the last line creates (page 63).

Teacher plays or reads the listening text about a girl who mixed up her shoes and her plate twice; students select the correct picture related to the narration (page 65) and then arrange the seven events in order of occurrence (page 66).
Teacher reviews the event-ordering answers on the board, then leads a brief discussion connecting the girl's preoccupied muddle to the funny man's inverted behaviour in the poem.
Teacher previews the speaking activity — telling a joke with correct voice — for the next period.

periods.teacher_notes: The poem_appreciation_summary notes that the final stanza is the 'perfect non-answer'; use the Extract 2 discussion to help students see that the funny man's world is genuinely and permanently inverted, not just a one-off joke. For listening, the transcript includes the detail of putting shoes in the kitchen — ensure students catch this for the sequencing task.

periods.activity_title: Telling a Joke and Writing a Limerick

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Tell the man-and-God joke with correct voice for each character
Write an original limerick using the AABBA structure and template

phases.description: Teacher models the man-and-God joke once with the three different voices, then pairs take turns performing it — narrator's matter-of-fact voice, man's louder respectful voice, God's deep pleasant voice (page 66).

Teacher explains the AABBA rhyme scheme using the example limericks, then students write their own limerick on the given template — introduction of a person or place, a silly story, and an unexpected ending (page 67).
Two or three students read their limericks aloud; teacher gives brief feedback on rhyme scheme adherence and previews the vocabulary work on phrasal verbs.

periods.teacher_notes: The speaking task links directly to the poem's comic spirit — the joke's punchline 'Wait a minute' is a verbal inversion similar to the funny man's behaviour. For the limerick, ensure students understand that lines 1, 2, and 5 rhyme (A) and lines 3 and 4 rhyme (B) before they begin writing.

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Match 'take' phrasal verbs to their correct meanings
Fill blanks using take up/after/in/over/off with correct tense
Arrange movement words in increasing order of pace

phases.description: Teacher introduces phrasal verbs using 'walking down', 'taking out', 'sat down' from the poem, then students match the five 'take' phrasal verbs to their meanings (page 64).

Students fill in the five blanks using the correct 'take' phrasal verb with appropriate tense changes (page 64). Teacher reviews answers on the board, highlighting tense adjustment in item 4 (took off).

Teacher introduces the movement-words scale — stagger, walk, hop, run — and students fill in the three blanks with jog, trot, sprint in increasing order of pace (page 65). Class discusses how these words appear in the poem.
Teacher notes that the Column 3 phrasal verb creation task (look/run/put/break/call) is an enrichment self-study activity, and previews Section C — the play 'Say the Right Thing'.

periods.teacher_notes: The movement-words task connects directly to the poem's comic physicality — 'walking down', 'staggered back', 'hopped home on his head'. The Column 3 task (creating new phrasal verbs for look/run/put/break/call) is not covered in class due to time; flag it as enrichment.

items.source_lo: Student analyses literary devices and tone in a nonsense poem.
items.item_stem:
Read the following stanza from the poem:

'He said, "Allow me to present
Your Highness with a rose."
And taking out a currant bun
He held it to my nose.'

The poet creates humour by having the man behave with perfect courtesy while doing something absurd. Identify ONE literary device used in this stanza and explain how it contributes to the comic tone of the poem.

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: The device is inversion (or incongruity): the man uses formal, polite language ('Allow me to present Your Highness') but substitutes an ordinary currant bun for the rose he has promised, creating absurd humour by combining high courtesy with a completely wrong object.

items.question_text:
Read the following stanza from the poem:

'He said, "Allow me to present
Your Highness with a rose."
And taking out a currant bun
He held it to my nose.'

The poet creates humour by having the man behave with perfect courtesy while doing something absurd. Identify ONE literary device used in this stanza and explain how it contributes to the comic tone of the poem.

items.source_lo: Student sequences events from a listened personal narrative in correct order.
items.item_stem: You will listen to a girl describe a funny incident that happened while she was preoccupied with preparing for a presentation. After listening, arrange the four events in the table in the order they occurred by writing 1, 2, 3, or 4 next to each.
items.visual_stimulus:
Event|Order
She removed her shoes at the door and went to the kitchen.|
She placed her plate in her room by mistake.|
She took her shoes to the kitchen instead of putting them away.|
She laughed at herself after realising what she had done.|

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: Correct order: (b) She removed her shoes at the door — 1; (a) She placed her plate in her room — 2; (d) She took her shoes to the kitchen — 3; (c) She laughed at herself — 4.

items.question_text: You will listen to a girl describe a funny incident that happened while she was preoccupied with preparing for a presentation. After listening, arrange the four events in the table in the order they occurred by writing 1, 2, 3, or 4 next to each.

items.source_lo: Student performs oral narration using varied voice to convey character.
items.item_stem: The poem 'A Funny Man' describes a character who behaves in an upside-down way but remains polite and cheerful. Prepare a short oral retelling (4–6 sentences) of the funny man's visit from the narrator's point of view. Use at least two different voices or tones — one for the narrator describing events, and one for the funny man's own words — and perform it for the class.

expected_elements: Student uses a distinct narrator's voice and a separate voice for the funny man.
Retelling covers at least two absurd actions from the poem.
Student conveys the comic, cheerful tone rather than a serious or confused one.
Delivery is clear enough for the class to follow the sequence of events.

items.question_text: The poem 'A Funny Man' describes a character who behaves in an upside-down way but remains polite and cheerful. Prepare a short oral retelling (4–6 sentences) of the funny man's visit from the narrator's point of view. Use at least two different voices or tones — one for the narrator describing events, and one for the funny man's own words — and perform it for the class.

items.source_lo: Student composes a limerick using the AABBA rhyme scheme.
items.item_stem:
Write a limerick about a funny character who, like the man in the poem, does everything the wrong way around. Your limerick must:
- be exactly five lines long
- follow the AABBA rhyme scheme (lines 1, 2, and 5 rhyme; lines 3 and 4 rhyme)
- introduce your character in line 1
- tell a silly story in lines 2–4
- end with an unexpected twist in line 5

expected_elements: Limerick is exactly five lines with AABBA rhyme scheme maintained.
Line 1 introduces a named character or place.
Lines 3 and 4 are shorter and rhyme with each other (BB pattern).
Line 5 contains an unexpected or funny conclusion.

items.question_text:
Write a limerick about a funny character who, like the man in the poem, does everything the wrong way around. Your limerick must:
- be exactly five lines long
- follow the AABBA rhyme scheme (lines 1, 2, and 5 rhyme; lines 3 and 4 rhyme)
- introduce your character in line 1
- tell a silly story in lines 2–4
- end with an unexpected twist in line 5

items.source_lo: Student identifies and uses phrasal verbs drawn from a poem's action language.
items.item_stem:
The poem describes the funny man 'walking down' the street, 'taking out' a currant bun, and sitting down on the ground. These are all phrasal verbs.

Fill in each blank below with the correct phrasal verb from the box. Change the tense if needed.

[take off  /  take after  /  take in  /  take over  /  take up]

(1) The new principal will _______ the management of the school from next month.
(2) Riya decided to _______ sketching as a hobby after seeing street art.
(3) The young comedian's act _______ so quickly that he was invited to perform in five cities.
(4) It was hard to _______ the meaning of the poem at first, but the class discussion helped.

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: (1) take over; (2) take up; (3) took off; (4) take in.

items.question_text:
The poem describes the funny man 'walking down' the street, 'taking out' a currant bun, and sitting down on the ground. These are all phrasal verbs.

Fill in each blank below with the correct phrasal verb from the box. Change the tense if needed.

[take off  /  take after  /  take in  /  take over  /  take up]

(1) The new principal will _______ the management of the school from next month.
(2) Riya decided to _______ sketching as a hobby after seeing street art.
(3) The young comedian's act _______ so quickly that he was invited to perform in five cities.
(4) It was hard to _______ the meaning of the poem at first, but the class discussion helped.
